one_line_takeaway: A reproducible, W3C PROV-O conformant corpus of 17,664 validated LLM-agent execution traces captured under prompt-injection attacks by passively instrumenting AgentDojo, built as a substrate for agent-provenance and injection-detection research rather than as a new attack or defense.
key_finding: This is a dataset artifact rather than an experimental-claims paper, so the headline 'finding' is the corpus itself and its reproducibility guarantees: 17,664 validated agent-execution traces (2,944 per model x 6 models), each with a PROV-O graph that passes both structural validation and round-trip isomorphism, and a derivation pipeline that decouples labelling/representation from model execution so the corpus can be re-derived offline without re-running any LLM. Per model the 2,944 traces decompose as 97 benign runs plus 949 security tasks x 3 attack strategies.
core_contribution: AgentDojo-PROV is a resource/dataset paper releasing a public corpus of W3C PROV-O conformant provenance graphs of LLM agent executions. Its own contribution is NOT the AgentDojo benchmark itself (that is prior work it instruments) but rather (1) a purely-observing capture layer bolted onto AgentDojo that records lossless execution transcripts without altering agent behavior, and (2) a deterministic pipeline that derives a standards-conformant (W3C PROV-O RDF) provenance graph from each transcript as a pure function, so any relabelling or representation change is an offline recompute rather than a costly model re-run. Every released graph is validated both structurally and via a PROV-JSON <-> PROV-O RDF round-trip isomorphism check, making the corpus a reproducible, machine-checkable substrate for downstream research on agent auditing, prompt-injection detection, and execution provenance.
threat_model: The corpus is not itself a defense; it observationally captures agent executions under the prompt-injection threat model inherited from AgentDojo. The adversary is an external party who plants malicious instructions in third-party data returned by an agent's tools (indirect prompt injection) or in direct input, aiming to hijack the tool-using LLM agent into performing attacker-chosen tasks (e.g., data exfiltration). The corpus records executions under three concrete attack strategies (direct, important_instructions, injecagent) alongside benign runs, with injection ground-truth labels, so provenance/detection methods can be trained and evaluated. The capture layer is explicitly non-intervening, so it introduces no new defense or attack surface of its own.
limitations: (1) Scope: limited to four AgentDojo suites (workspace, banking, travel, slack) and three attack strategies (direct, important_instructions, injecagent); generalization to other environments/attacks is untested. (2) It is an observational corpus, not a detector or defense, and provides no baseline detection/attribution results in the abstract. (3) It is a point-in-time snapshot of specific pinned model versions (e.g., DeepSeek-V3.2, GPT-5-nano, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Llama 3.3 70B, Qwen2.5 72B, GLM-4.6) and the pinned agentdojo 0.1.35 / suite v1.2.2, so it will drift as models and the benchmark evolve. (4) GROUNDING CAVEAT: these limitations are inferred from the abstract only; the linked eprint PDF is a different paper (the original AgentDojo), so limitations stated in the corpus paper's own full text could not be verified.

datasets_used: AgentDojo (agentdojo 0.1.35; suite set v1.2.2) with the four suites: workspace, banking, travel, slack -- the benchmark that is instrumented (prior work, the substrate for this corpus)
The released AgentDojo-PROV corpus itself: PROV-O provenance graphs plus lossless transcripts (this paper's own artifact)
quantitative_results: 6 agent models: DeepSeek-V3.2 (deepseek-chat), OpenAI GPT-5-nano (gpt-5-nano), Google Gemini 2.5 Flash (gemini-2.5-flash), Meta Llama 3.3 70B (llama-3.3-70b-instruct), Alibaba Qwen2.5 72B (qwen2.5-72b-instruct), Z.ai GLM-4.6 (glm-4.6)
Per-model composition: 97 benign traces + 949 security tasks x 3 attack strategies (direct, important_instructions, injecagent)
Every released graph passes structural validation AND a PROV-JSON <-> PROV-O RDF round-trip isomorphism check
baselines_compared: None reported in the abstract -- this is a corpus/resource paper, not a comparative evaluation; it provides no detection/defense baselines
